overview.title: 300,000 people. 104 matches. One system.
overview.description: Explore who requested, paid for, designed, manufactured, regulated, and operated each piece of the World Cup.

areas.title: The World Cup, split by systems.
areas.description: Open any area without hunting through the swarm. Each domain connects people, organizations, processes, countries, and evidence.

matches.title: A match is a temporary city.
matches.description: Pick a matchday moment and the map will automatically focus the organizations, people, and processes that make it possible.

venues.title: The host-city brand does not always match the real city.
venues.description: Dallas plays in Arlington, Boston in Foxborough, and San Francisco Bay Area in Santa Clara. Open a venue to see who owns, operates, pays, and regulates.

people.title: There is no single people number.
people.description: We separate accredited staff, expanded operations, and the global chain to avoid double-counting people who held more than one role.

evidence.title: Atlas reference index.
evidence.description: Each point appears as an independent block with its sources linked one under another. Confidence colors stay visible on the map.

cities.interface: City of Toronto FIFA World Cup 2026 municipal secretariat
cities.ownerOperator: City of Toronto · operated by MLSE
cities.mobility: TTC, Metrolinx/GO, UP Express, and MTO
cities.sourceLabel: City of Toronto · World Cup documentation

leaders: Distributed public leadership; no single CEO
cities.finance: Estimated net provincial cost CAD 90–114 million

cities.mobility: Metro, Metrobús, Light Rail, RTP, AICM, and AIFA
cities.finance: Public opening-match operations; distributed urban budget

leaders: Juan José Frangie · designated public lead
cities.ownerOperator: Private venue linked to Chivas/Grupo Omnilife
cities.mobility: SITEUR, Mi Macro, buses, and shuttles

cities.finance: Line items split across state, municipalities, and operator

cities.interface: Atlanta World Cup Host Committee / Atlanta Sports Council
cities.finance: State funds and federal grants; total not consolidated

cities.mobility: MBTA commuter rail y buses expresos
cities.finance: Local security reported; full budget not published

leaders: Dan Hunt y Nina Vaca · Co-chairs
cities.ownerOperator: City of Arlington · Dallas Cowboys bajo lease
cities.mobility: DART, TRE, Trinity Metro, shuttles y GoPass
cities.finance: USD 51.5m FEMA + USD 10.03m FTA; also hosts the IBC
USD 9.092m FTA; security in separate line items

leaders: Clark Hunt y Cliff Illig · Honorary Co-chairs
cities.ownerOperator: Jackson County Sports Complex Authority + Chiefs

cities.interface: LA World Cup Host Committee / LASEC

cities.finance: Approved services and subsidies; close-out pending

cities.publicLayer: New Jersey + New York + autoridades regionales
cities.mobility: NJ TRANSIT, MTA, buses y shuttles

cities.finance: USD 8.47m FTA + separate security and contributions

cities.ownerOperator: Washington State Public Stadium Authority + First & Goal
cities.mobility: Sound Transit, King County Metro y ferries

nodes.label: World Cup 2026
nodes.summary: A temporary global organization connecting competition, governments, stadiums, technology, suppliers, media, and public services.
facts: 16 venues in Canada, Mexico, and the United States

peopleNamed.name: Heimo Schirgi
peopleNamed.role: Chief Operating Officer, FIFA World Cup 2026

nodes.label: Competition
nodes.summary: Each match is an ephemeral enterprise: it starts days earlier and ends after financial, technical, and security close-out.
facts: Operational anatomy from −72 hours through demobilization

peopleNamed.name: Pierluigi Collina
peopleNamed.role: Chair of the FIFA Referees Committee

nodes.label: Security
nodes.summary: FIFA Safety & Security, stewards, private firms, police, intelligence, traffic, counter-drone, fire, and emergency services.
nodes.people: 20,000+ security personnel confirmed in the group stage

nodes.label: Broadcast
nodes.summary: HBS produces the host feed; dozens of Media Rights Licensees customize and distribute it worldwide.
nodes.people: Up to 3,400 people per day at the IBC
facts: More than 56 media partners inside the IBC

nodes.label: Stadiums
nodes.summary: Public and private buildings, standing operators, and a FIFA control period that fully transforms how they run.
facts: 300,000 m² of turf across venues and training sites

nodes.label: Teams
nodes.summary: Players and technical staff travel inside a logistics bubble of hotels, base camps, kit vans, security, and transport.
facts: 6,310 transfers accumulated at the research cut-off

nodes.label: Ceremonies
nodes.summary: Flags, music, artists, pyrotechnics, protocol, and technology turn minutes of ceremony into months of production.
nodes.people: Around 140 people visible for two giant flags alone
facts: Balich Wonder Studio on main ceremonies

nodes.label: Refereeing technology
nodes.summary: Cameras, fiber, connected ball, AI, cybersecurity, and refereeing systems form a critical distributed platform.
nodes.people: 1,500–4,000 people in central engineering and support
facts: 30 dedicated technical cameras per stadium for SAOT + GLT

nodes.label: Hospitality
nodes.summary: Tickets, suites, hotels, food, accessibility, retail, and guest care form another enterprise inside the tournament.

nodes.label: Governance
nodes.summary: FIFA, FWC26, host associations, Host Cities, and governments coordinate a temporary organization with multiple decision centers.
nodes.people: Estimate: 2,000–6,000 direction, PMO, command-centre, and public-coordination roles
facts: 16 local interfaces with different legal forms
sources: FIFA · Tournament Operations Centre and Intelligent Command Centre

nodes.label: Enterprise technology
nodes.summary: Private networks, cloud, identity, accreditation, devices, workforce systems, and monitoring centers keep the tournament connected.
nodes.people: Estimate: 1,500–4,000 people; overlap with broadcast and FIFA workforce
facts: Systems across more than 600 official sites

nodes.label: Health and medicine
nodes.summary: Team and referee medicine, first aid, ambulances, hospitals, anti-doping, public health, and climate protocols.
nodes.people: Estimate: 8,000–20,000 medical professionals and roles in the expanded tournament
facts: Separate layers for participants and the public

nodes.label: Transport and logistics
nodes.summary: Borders, aviation, customs, buses, public transit, shuttles, depots, freight, and contingency routes.
nodes.people: Estimate: 25,000–80,000 roles across mobility, airports, freight, and traffic
facts: Public and private networks coordinated per venue
sources: FIFA · Rock-it Cargo, official logistics provider

nodes.label: Accommodation and food
nodes.summary: Hotels, rooming, kitchens, sports nutrition, cleaning, laundry, food traceability, and waste.
nodes.people: Estimate: 40,000–120,000 people in lodging, food, and related services
facts: Differentiated hotels for teams, referees, FIFA, and media

nodes.label: Fans and accessibility
nodes.summary: Ticketing, payments, fraud, entry, Fan Festivals, multilingual information, mobility, and accessibility services.
nodes.people: Estimate: 15,000–40,000 direct roles in ticketing, fan services, and accessibility

nodes.label: Brand and content
nodes.summary: Brand architecture, emblem, posters, music, campaigns, content, creators, city dressing, and clean zones.
nodes.people: Estimate: 5,000–20,000 people in the global creative-production chain
facts: Shared system with 16 Host City expressions

nodes.label: Sponsors and retail
nodes.summary: Sponsorships, licensing, activations, equipment, ball, products, manufacturing, stores, payments, and royalties.
nodes.people: Estimate: 20,000–100,000 people in activations, manufacturing, distribution, and retail

nodes.label: Sustainability & human rights
nodes.summary: Climate, energy, water, waste, responsible sourcing, labor, inclusion, grievance mechanisms, community, and legacy.
nodes.people: Estimate: 2,000–8,000 specialized roles and functional liaisons
facts: Social, environmental, economic, and governance strategy
Specific plans per Host City and venue
Sustainable Sourcing Code for the supply chain

nodes.label: Finance and legal
nodes.summary: CFO, budget, procurement, contracts, tax, insurance, privacy, permits, payments, reconciliation, and audit.
nodes.people: Estimate: 3,000–12,000 cross-cutting roles; many overlap other branches
facts: Separate circuits in FIFA, FWC26, Host Cities, and governments
Financial close-out continues after the final match
